assertiveness_why: Harry asks structured, well-prepared multi-part questions and follows up with genuine curiosity (e.g., pressing on whether a 10x-better product must also be cheaper), but never contests or challenges Sarah's points.
question_style: Open questions built around her career arc (Bessemer to Pinterest to Greylock) with natural follow-ups; he occasionally injects a light challenge framed as a clarifying question ('does it always have to be a financial reward too?') but does not push back or interrupt.
tone: Warm, matey, and playful, with running jokes (Snapchat, getting lost with Google Maps) and clear personal rapport; enthusiastic but not purely fawning.
depth: Goes beyond surface into VC personal-branding strategy (blog vs. Medium, 'freemium model' framing), the 10x-better-and-cheaper investment framework, and how she evaluates founder-market fit; reasonably substantive for the era.
harry_quotes: What made you know it was special when you saw it?
Does it have to save money, out of interest?
How do you communicate with your founders?
learnings: Tavel evaluates consumer/marketplace opportunities through a '10x better and cheaper' framework, arguing durable multi-billion-dollar companies must either be free or dramatically cheaper than alternatives.
She rejects the 'founder friendly' label, framing her role instead as being a truth-seeker who challenges founders' assumptions rather than simply saying yes.
She treats public writing/blogging as a VC 'freemium model' -- giving founders enough exposure to her thinking to make an informed choice of investor, much like a free product tier.
She tracked Pinterest's early traction via Alexa rankings against competitors before investing, converting qualitative excitement about the product into quantitative conviction.
notable: Part of a dedicated Greylock 'feature week' format, with the episode explicitly teasing the next guest (a Greylock-backed founder) to close out the theme.
